stem: A 45-45-90 triangle has legs of length 5. What is the length of the hypotenuse? (Enter as a decimal, rounded to the nearest hundredth.)
solutionStandard: Hypotenuse \(= 5\sqrt{2} \approx 7.07\).

stem: In the figure below, what is the length of side \(AB\) (the long leg)?
solutionStandard: This is a 30-60-90 triangle with hypotenuse 8, so the short leg is \(8/2=4\) (matches the given short leg), and the long leg is \(4\sqrt{3}\).

stem: A 30-60-90 triangle has a short leg of length 6. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
solutionStandard: The hypotenuse is always twice the short leg: \(2 \times 6 = 12\).

stem: A square has a diagonal of length \(10\sqrt{2}\). What is the length of a side?
solutionStandard: A square's diagonal splits it into two 45-45-90 triangles, with diagonal \(=\text{side}\times\sqrt{2}\). So side \(=10\sqrt2/\sqrt2=10\).

stem: An equilateral triangle has side length 10. An altitude is drawn from one vertex to the opposite side.
solutionStandard: The altitude splits the equilateral triangle into two 30-60-90 triangles with hypotenuse 10 and short leg 5, so the altitude (long leg) is \(5\sqrt3\approx8.66\). Since \(8.66>8.5\), Quantity A is greater.

stem: A 45-45-90 triangle has a hypotenuse of length \(12\sqrt{2}\). What is the length of each leg?
solutionStandard: Leg \(= \text{hypotenuse}/\sqrt2 = 12\sqrt2/\sqrt2=12\).

stem: What is the area of an equilateral triangle with side length 8?
solutionStandard: The altitude of an equilateral triangle with side \(s\) is \(\frac{s\sqrt3}{2}\); for \(s=8\), altitude \(=4\sqrt3\). Area \(=\frac{1}{2}(8)(4\sqrt3)=16\sqrt3\).

stem: Which of the following triangles must be 45-45-90? Select all that apply.

options.text: A right triangle with two equal legs
A right triangle with a 30° angle

solutionStandard: (A), (B), and (C) all describe the same shape: a right triangle with two equal legs (and therefore two 45° angles). (D) describes a 30-60-90 triangle instead.

stem: A ladder 20 feet long leans against a wall, making a 60° angle with the ground. How high up the wall does the ladder reach?
solutionStandard: The ladder is the hypotenuse of a 30-60-90 triangle (angles 60° at the ground, 30° at the top, 90° at the wall). The height is the long leg, opposite the 60° angle: with hypotenuse 20, the short leg (opposite 30°, along the ground) is 10, and the long leg (height) is \(10\sqrt3\).

stem: A rectangle has length 10 and width 10. What is the length of its diagonal? (Enter as \(k\sqrt{2}\); give just the value of \(k\).)
solutionStandard: Since length equals width, the diagonal splits the rectangle (a square in this case) into 45-45-90 triangles: diagonal \(=10\sqrt2\), so \(k=10\).

stem: A 45-45-90 triangle has legs of length {{a}}. The hypotenuse can be written as \(k\sqrt{2}\). What is the value of \(k\)?
solutionStandard: For a 45-45-90 triangle, hypotenuse \(= \text{leg} \times \sqrt2 = {{a}}\sqrt2\), so \(k={{a}}\).

stem: In the figure, what is the length of the hypotenuse \(x\)?
solutionStandard: Both legs equal 7, so this is a 45-45-90 triangle: hypotenuse \(=7\sqrt2\).

stem: A 30-60-90 triangle has a short leg of length {{a}}. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
solutionStandard: The hypotenuse is always twice the short leg: \(2 \times {{a}} = {{2*a}}\).

stem: A square has a side length of 9. What is the length of its diagonal?
solutionStandard: A square's diagonal \(= \text{side} \times \sqrt2 = 9\sqrt2\).

stem: An equilateral triangle has side length {{s}}. An altitude is drawn from one vertex to the opposite side.
solutionStandard: The altitude of an equilateral triangle with side \(s\) is \(\dfrac{s\sqrt3}{2} \approx 0.866s\), which is always greater than \(0.85s\) (Quantity B), since \(\sqrt3/2>0.85\).

stem: What is the area of an equilateral triangle with side length 6?
solutionStandard: Altitude \(=\dfrac{6\sqrt3}{2}=3\sqrt3\). Area \(=\dfrac{1}{2}(6)(3\sqrt3)=9\sqrt3\).

stem: A 45-45-90 triangle has a hypotenuse of length \({{a}}\sqrt{2}\). What is the length of each leg?
solutionStandard: Leg \(= \text{hypotenuse}/\sqrt2 = {{a}}\sqrt2/\sqrt2={{a}}\).

stem: A ladder 16 feet long leans against a wall, making a 30° angle with the ground. How high up the wall does the ladder reach?
solutionStandard: The ladder is the hypotenuse of a 30-60-90 triangle. The height is opposite the 30° angle (at the ground), which is always the short leg: \(16/2=8\).

stem: A 30-60-90 triangle has a hypotenuse of length {{2*a}}. The area of the triangle can be written as \(k\sqrt{3}\). What is the value of \(k\)?
solutionStandard: Short leg \(={{a}}\), long leg \(={{a}}\sqrt3\). Area \(=\dfrac{1}{2}({{a}})({{a}}\sqrt3) = \dfrac{ {{a*a}} }{2}\sqrt3\), so \(k={{(a*a)/2}}\).

stem: A regular hexagon can be divided into 6 equilateral triangles, each with side length 4.
solutionStandard: Each equilateral triangle has area \(\dfrac{\sqrt3}{4}(4^2)=4\sqrt3\approx6.93\). The hexagon's area is \(6\times4\sqrt3=24\sqrt3\approx41.6\). Since \(41.6>40\), Quantity A is greater.
